preamble: The shift from [gʱ] to [g] often occurs as a result of Grassmann's Law, a process of dissimilatory deaspiration in Ancient Greek and Sanskrit. If an aspirated consonant is followed by another aspirated consonant in the next syllable, the first one loses its aspiration.

examples.note: Grassmann's Law: The first [gʱ] in the reduplicated perfect of 'gʱas-' (to eat) deaspirates to [g] because it is followed by another [gʱ].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
